coredata.dc:title: Mechanism of ammonium adsorption onto the surface of heteroatom doped graphene quantum dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Kumar, Saurav
Agnihotri, Neha
coredata.dc:description: The adsorption mechanism of ammonium (NH4 +) ion onto graphene quantum dots (GQDs) surface, modified with vacancy and heteroatom (nitrogen (N) and oxygen (O)), has been studied using a combination of dispersion-corrected density functional theory (DFT) and quantum theory of atoms in molecules (QTAIM) approaches in aqueous media. The potential of GQD-NX and GQD-OX surfaces as highly effective adsorbents for NH4 + ions has been explored by detailed analysis of adsorption energies, molecular electrostatic potential, charge transfer, density-of-states, non-covalent interaction, and desorption time. It has been observed that the adsorption of NH4 + ions on the GQD-NX surfaces is primarily chemisorption, governed by electrostatic interactions and hydrogen bonds. However, adsorption of NH4 + over the surface of GQD-OX predominantly ranges from strong physisorption to weak chemisorption arising from van der Waals interactions and hydrogen bonds. These findings present compelling new approach utilizing modified GQDs as highly efficient adsorbents for removing NH4 + ions from water.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Adsorption
Physisorption
Chemisorption
DFT
QTAIM

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Structural and electronic properties of the adsorbents
NH4 + ion adsorption onto the adsorbents
Quantum theory of atoms in molecules analysis

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors thank the Science and Engineering Research Board (SERB), New Delhi, India, for financial support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Optimized structures of (a) GQD-N1, (b) GQD-N2, (c) GQD-N3, (d) GQD-N4, (e) GQD-N5, (f) GQD-N6, (m) GQD-O1 (n) GQD-O2, (o) GQD-O3, (p) GQD-O4, (q) GQD-O5, (r) GQD-O6 along with MEP surfaces of (g) GQD-N1, (h) GQD-N2, (i) GQD-N3, (j) GQD-N4, (k) GQD-N5, (l) GQD-N6, (s) GQD-O1, (t) GQD-O2, (u) GQD-O3, (v) GQD-O4, (w) GQD-O5, (x) GQD-O6.
Optimized structures and adsorption energies (Eads) (eV) for the adsorption of NH4 + ion at the surface of GQD-NX.
Optimized structures and adsorption energies (Eads) (eV) for the adsorption of NH4 + ion at the surface of GQD-OX.

The visual representation of non-covalent interaction (NCI) between NH4 + ion and (a) GQD-N1, (b) GQD-N2, (c) GQD-N3, (d) GQD-N4, (e) GQD-N5, and (f) GQD-N6 by the 3D iso-surface. The plots of the reduced density gradient (s) versus the sign of the second eigen value of the electron-density Hessian matrix (λ 2) times electron density (sign(λ 2) ρ) for the adsorption of NH4 + ion at the surface of (g) GQD-N1, (h) GQD-N2, (i) GQD-N3, (j) GQD-N4, (k) GQD-N5, and (l) GQD-N6.

The visual representation of non-covalent interaction (NCI) between NH4 + ion and (a) GQD-O1, (b) GQD-O2, (c) GQD-O3, (d) GQD-O4, (e) GQD-O5, and (f) GQD-O6 by the 3D iso-surface. The plots of the reduced density gradient (s) versus the sign of the second eigen value of the electron-density Hessian matrix (λ 2) times electron density (sign(λ 2) ρ) for the adsorption of NH4 + ion at the surface of (g) GQD-O1, (h) GQD-O2, (i) GQD-O3, (j) GQD-O4, (k) GQD-O5, and (l) GQD-O6.

Total density of states (TDOS) and partial density of states (PDOS) of NH4 + ion onto the surface of the adsorbents (a) GQD-N1, (b) GQD-N2, (c) GQD-N3, (d) GQD-N4, (e) GQD-N5, (f) GQD-N6, (g) GQD-O1 (h) GQD-O2, (i) GQD-O3, (j) GQD-O4, (k) GQD-O5, (l) GQD-O6. The dotted line represents the HOMO orbital.
Recovery time (τ) for the desorption of the NH4 + ion from the surface of the adsorbents GQD-NX and GQD-OX obtained at the temperature 298 K.

The ADCH charges on nitrogen and oxygen atoms before ammonium ion (NH4 +) adsorption onto GQD-NX and GQD-OX, respectively, obtained at the CAM-B3LYP-D3/6–31 G(d,p) level of theory in aqueous media. A change in ADCH charge after the adsorption of NH4 + ion has also been reported.
Description of topological parameters at bond critical points (BCP) after the adsorption of NH4⁺ ion onto the surface of GQD-NX obtained by QTAIM analysis.
Description of topological parameters at bond critical points (BCP) after the adsorption of NH4⁺ ion onto the surface of GQD-OX obtained by QTAIM analysis.

head.ce:title: Mechanism of ammonium adsorption onto the surface of heteroatom doped graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, National Institute of Technology, Jamshedpur 831014, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Physics, National Institute of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, National Institute of Technology, Jamshedpur-831014, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The adsorption mechanism of ammonium (NH4 +) ion onto graphene quantum dots (GQDs) surface, modified with vacancy and heteroatom (nitrogen (N) and oxygen (O)), has been studied using a combination of dispersion-corrected density functional theory (DFT) and quantum theory of atoms in molecules (QTAIM) approaches in aqueous media. The potential of GQD-NX and GQD-OX surfaces as highly effective adsorbents for NH4 + ions has been explored by detailed analysis of adsorption energies, molecular electrostatic potential, charge transfer, density-of-states, non-covalent interaction, and desorption time. It has been observed that the adsorption of NH4 + ions on the GQD-NX surfaces is primarily chemisorption, governed by electrostatic interactions and hydrogen bonds. However, adsorption of NH4 + over the surface of GQD-OX predominantly ranges from strong physisorption to weak chemisorption arising from van der Waals interactions and hydrogen bonds. These findings present compelling new approach utilizing modified GQDs as highly efficient adsorbents for removing NH4 + ions from water.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The presence of ammonium ion (NH4 +) is a common occurrence in wastewater, often resulting from improper disposal of industrial, agricultural, and domestic waste. As society continues to progress through advancements in industry and agriculture, as well as living standards, the number of nitrogen-containing compounds released has significantly risen. This has led to an overabundance of NH4 + content in water, as ammonia (NH3) nitrogen gets easily dissolved and forms NH4 + ions. The pH level of water plays a crucial role in determining the ratio of NH4 +/NH3 concentration in water, with NH4 + being the dominant species at a pH value less than 9.25 [1]. Eutrophication, caused by an excess of NH4 + in water, can result in the loss of many aquatic species [2]. Additionally, consuming NH4 + beyond the body's detoxification capabilities can be harmful and toxic to human health [3]. Various methods, including membrane filtration, biological treatment, and chemical oxidation, can remove NH4 + ions from water [2]. However, the World Health Organization (WHO) recommend the use of adsorption technology as an inexpensive and environmentally friendly approach to efficiently removing NH4 + ions. The adsorbents can be reused, thus significantly promoting environmental sustainability [4]. Naturally activated adsorbents such as zeolite, pine sawdust, and biochar are commonly used examples [5–7]. In an effort to enhance NH4 + ion adsorption on carbonaceous surfaces from water, Yin et al. [4,8] investigated the impact of structural defects and oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface. For optimal NH4 + adsorption, adsorbents should have a high surface area, chemical stability, and multiple active sites for trapping ions.

Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) possess a non-zero band gap and are widely used as an adsorbent for improved molecular adsorption. Their abundant pore structure, significant surface area, high thermodynamic and mechanical stability, as well as favourable electrical conductivity, all contribute to their effectiveness [9,10]. The GQDs also exhibit exceptional tunability of electron mobility due to the quantum confinement effect, resulting in increased catalytic activity, electronic and magnetic properties, as shown in various theoretical and experimental investigations [11,12]. However, the molecules tend to weakly adhere to the pristine GQDs' surface through van der Waals (vdW) forces due to sp2 hybridization and π electron delocalization. Nevertheless, modifications through substitutional doping and vacancy creations have the potential to significantly impact the adsorption capability of GQDs.

Numerous successful investigations have been conducted on surface defects in graphene, such as substitutional doping, vacancy defects, and Stone-Wales defects [13]. Recently, researchers have demonstrated the valuable strategy of introducing vacancy-type defects in GQDs to possible sites to interact with cadmium (Cd), mercury (Hg), and lead (Pb) [14]. This has a profound influence on both the adsorption mechanism and the transfer of charge. However, these vacancies or defects can be stabilized through self-governing restoration mechanisms or via the utilization of passivating agents [15,16]. Various passivating agents such as hydrogen (H), boron (B), nitrogen (N), oxygen (O), fluorine (F), and sulphur (S) has been reported in recent literature for their role in stabilizing vacancies and serving as active sites to enhance the adsorption of various adsorbates [17–22]. Nevertheless, the difference in electronegativity between the passivating agent and carbon (C) atoms results in charge polarization within the nanopore, creating a potential well for both positive and negative ions. Following the experimental synthesis of O- and N-passivated nanoporous graphene, several research groups have explored its potential for various applications such as gas detection and metal trapping [23,24]. Ghenaatian et al. [20] investigated the adsorption capacity of toxic heavy metals (THMs) such as Cd, Hg, and Pb on GQDs as well as on N-coordinated defected GQDs, and found that incorporating N atom onto the GQD surface enhances the charge transfer and adsorption energy values for THMs over the surface of GQDs.

Additionally, a study conducted by Ghenaatian et al. [19] delved into the exceptional adsorption capabilities of O-passivated nanopores of various sizes in graphene nanoflakes (GNF) using Density Functional theory (DFT) to eliminate THM ions. Similarly, Ghosh and colleagues [25] illustrated the effective capture of different polar toxic and greenhouse gas molecules on the pyridinic/pyrrolic N-doped graphene surfaces at ambient temperature. Notably, the adsorption of gas molecules does not entail any chemical bond formation with the adsorbates; instead, it involves the establishment of hydrogen bonds between the N atoms of the adsorbents and the H atoms of the adsorbates. Nath et al. [26] utilized DFT and ab initio molecular dynamics (AIMD) techniques to assess the potential of pyridinic-rich N-doped graphene (PNG) for sulphur dioxide (SO2) detection by thoroughly examining adsorption energy, electronic properties, charge transfer, and recovery time characteristics. More recently, Krishnakumar et al. [27] conducted a comparative analysis on N-based azacrown ether-incorporated graphene nanopores and O-based crown ether-incorporated graphene nanopores in terms of their reactivity towards HF, H2O, and NH3 gas molecule adsorption. The bonding mechanisms between the N-doped GCEs and O-based GCEs with the gas molecules involve electrostatic, dispersion, and induction interactions. Further, in their efforts to synthesize sensitive electrochemical and fluorescence sensors for sodium and potassium ions, Iannazzo et al. [28] recently investigated the association of the electrical and optical characteristics of GQDs with the selective chelating property of the crown ethers 15-crown-5 and 18-crown-6. It has been recently reported that introducing N doping along with a double vacancy defect enhances the adsorption strength of GQDs towards toxic gases. This enhanced adsorption is primarily due to the presence of hydrogen bonds between the H atom in the adsorbate and the N atom in the adsorbent. [29,30]

The current literature lacks a comprehensive analysis proposing a state-of-the-art method for utilizing modified GQDs as highly effective adsorbents for removing NH4 + ions from water. The present study aims to investigate the adsorption mechanism of NH4 + ions onto GQDs modified with vacancy and doped with N and O atoms using DFT in aqueous media. The objective is to evaluate the stability of the adsorbate-adsorbent system by examining geometric parameters, adsorption energy, molecular electrostatic potential (MEP), and atomic dipole-corrected Hirshfeld (ADCH) charge analysis. In addition, a detailed study has been conducted elucidating the binding affinity, density-of-states (DOS), desorption time, nature of interactions through non-covalent interactions (NCI), reduced density gradient (RDG), and quantum theory of atoms in molecules (QTAIM) analysis.

DFT methodology was utilized for all computational analysis due to its balance between the computational cost and accuracy. The geometry optimization of all the interactions has been carried out at the CAM‐B3LYP‐D3/6‐31 G(d,p) level of theory in aqueous media. An exchange-correlation functional known as CAM-B3LYP integrates accurate Hartee-Fock exchange over long-range interactions. To further ensure accurate reflection of dispersion correction in the adsorption energies, Grimme's empirical dispersion-correction (D3) has also been incorporated along with the CAM-B3LYP functional [31]. Additionally, to mimic the effect of aqueous environment, all the structures have been optimized in water solvent, using the integral equation formalism polarizable continuum model (IEF-PCM) of self-consistent reaction field theory (SCRF). All the structures are found to be true minima on the potential energy surface which was further confirmed by vibrational frequency analysis in aqueous media. All the vibrational frequencies are found to be real. All the calculations have been carried out using the Gaussian 16 [32].

MEP isosurfaces have been visualized using Gaussview 6 [33]. ADCH charge analysis has been performed using the Multiwfn [8,34,35]. It examines the atomic charge distribution and transfer between adsorbent and adsorbate. Further, Multiwfn code, has also been employed to perform DOS, QTAIM and NCI analyses, offering a comprehensive understanding of the adsorption behaviour of the adsorbate over the adsorbent surface. Visual Molecular Dynamics (VMD) [36] and the Gnuplot platform [37] are used to obtain visual representations of the NCI 3D iso-surface plots and the 2D-RDG scatter plot, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and electronic properties of the adsorbents
ce:section.ce:para: Circumcircumcoronene (C96H24) has been employed as a model system for graphene quantum dots (GQDs). This molecule comprises 96 carbon atoms arranged in hexagonal rings, with terminal passivation provided by hydrogen atoms. GQD-NX (X = 1–6) and GQD-OX (X = 1–6) have been created through substitutional doping of carbon atoms with nitrogen (N) and oxygen (O) atoms, as well as through the removal of one, two, three, and six carbon atoms from C96H24. Specifically, GQD-N1/GQD-O1 has been obtained by substituting a carbon atom with a heteroatom (N or O) and removing a carbon atom, while GQD-N2/GQD-O2 was generated by substituting two carbon atoms with heteroatoms (N or O). Substituting three carbon atoms with heteroatoms and removing a carbon atom produced GQD-N3/GQD-O3. GQD-N4/GQD-O4, GQD-N5/GQD-O5, and GQD-N6/GQD-O6 have been produced by substituting four, five, and six carbon atoms with heteroatoms and removing two, three, and six carbon atoms, respectively.

Fig. 1(a-f) and Fig. 1(m-r) provide the optimized geometries of GQDs surface modified with vacancy and doping with N and O heteroatom respectively obtained at the CAM-B3LYP-D3/6–31 G(d,p) level of theory in aqueous media. In the case of pristine GQDs, the carbon-carbon (C-C) bond length is nearly 1.41 Å. However, in the vacancy modified and N-doped graphene quantum dots (GQD-NX), the nitrogen-carbon (N-C) bond lengths range from 1.30 Å to 1.40 Å (Fig. 1(a-f)). Similarly, in the vacancy modified and O-doped GQDs (GQD-OX), the oxygen-carbon (O-C) bond length varies between 1.31 Å and 1.39 Å (Fig. 1(m-r)). Further, ammonia (NH3) readily dissolves in water, forming the adsorbate NH4 + ion. The theoretical model of the NH4 + ion involves a N atom bonded to four H atoms, with an added positive charge. The bond length between N atom and H atom is approximately 1.02 Å, and the bond angle between H-N-H atoms is approximately 109.47°.

Further, the ADCH charges have been calculated on N and O atoms doped into GQD-NX and GQD-OX, respectively. ADCH charge analysis is a fundamental technique for analysing a molecule's basic characteristics, potential reaction direction, and atom-by-atom state. This method's relevance, quick computation time, and consistency with charges obtained by the electrostatic potential fitting making it widely used in examining charge distribution and transfer in molecules [8,35,37]. The corresponding charge values have been provided in Table 1. It is evident from Table 1 that the doped N atoms in GQD-NX carry negative charges, indicating their ability to attract positively charged ions. As the nitrogen content in the GQDs increases, the charges also increase from −0.2e to −0.31e. These results show that the varying N concentration from N1 to N6 on the defect sites influences the charge values on the active sites. Notably, the active site of GQD-N6 exhibits a significantly higher negative charge than other GQD-NX, suggesting its substantial impact on the adsorption of NH4 + ions. Similar to GQD-NX, the ADCH charge values of the active sites in GQD-OX are also influenced by the O atom distribution on the defect sites. As the concentration of O atom increases, the charges also increase from −0.042e to −0.162e. GQD-O6 displays an even charge distribution among all six oxygen atoms, with a value of −0.162e. Comprehensive analysis shows that GQD-N6 exhibits the highest reactivity as an adsorbent towards NH4 + ion adsorption when compared to other GQD-NX and GQD-OX. This is attributed to its substantial negative charge of approximately −0.313e.

Moreover, the molecular electrostatic potential (MEP) surfaces visually depict the three-dimensional charge distribution on a molecule's surface. These surfaces enable observing varying charge density regions, offering valuable insights into the molecule's electron-donating and accepting reactivity regions. In Fig. 1(g-l) and Fig. 1(s-x), red corresponds to areas of negative charge density. At the same time, blue indicates regions of positive charge density on the MEP surfaces at an isovalue of 0.0004 a.u. Fig. S1(d) illustrates a homogeneous distribution of negative charges across the pristine GQD surface, indicating limited reactive sites for adsorbate adsorption. The pristine GQD exhibits a lack of reactivity attributed to its strong sp2 and π-electron conjugation, leading to a weak adsorption interaction between GQD and the adsorbate[38]. In contrast, incorporating heteroatoms (N and O) and creating vacancies disrupt the uniform electron distribution, establishing electrochemically active sites. Hence, introducing nitrogen/oxygen/vacancy defects enhances charge accumulation around the active area, leading to efficient detection of pollutants from the surrounding environment [25,26,29,30,39]. Molecular electrostatic potential (MEP) surfaces of GQD-NX and GQD-OX, given in Fig. 1(g-l) and Fig. 1(s-x), illustrate the changes in electronic distribution resulting from the modification of GQDs’ surface through vacancy and doping with heteroatom (N or O). As depicted in Fig. 1(g-l), the effective charges disperse more widely around the defects with nitrogen content increasing, forming active centres, particularly noticeable from GQD-N3 to GQD-N6. These findings are supported by the charge distribution outlined in the ADCH analysis presented in Table 1. The negatively charged active centres surrounding the defects facilitate stronger interactions between GQD-NX and positively charged NH4 + ion. A similar pattern is observed in GQD-OX, as demonstrated in Fig. 1(s-x). However, the ADCH analysis in Table 1 indicates that the charge distribution in GQD-OX is less dispersed than in GQD-NX, suggesting weaker interaction strength for GQD-OX adsorbents. Additionally, In the MEP surfaces of GQD-N6 and GQD-O6, a notable observation is the creation of a nanopore containing a neutrally charged region at its centre. This active region need further investigation for the transportation of diverse ions and pollutants through the nanopores in GQD-N6 and GQD-O6 for a range of ion filtration and separation purposes. Though several research groups have employed these nanopores for various applications, a comprehensive analysis remains a topic for further investigation.

The electrostatic potential diagram for NH4 + ion is entirely blue because the NH4 + ion in the aqueous media possesses a positive charge, as shown in Fig. S1(b). Positively charged NH4 + ions are readily attracted to the negatively charged GQDs. In other words, the red area of the GQDs has a propensity for adsorbing NH4 + ion. Thus, the MEP surface analysis results clearly indicate the adsorption sites. These analyses conclusively show that choosing the adsorption site with red and orange region yields prompt and precise results towards the NH4 + ion adsorption.

ce:section.ce:section-title: NH4 + ion adsorption onto the adsorbents
ce:section.ce:para:
To determine the equilibrium structures and positions of the adsorbed NH4 + ion onto the surface of GQD-NX and GQD-OX, geometry optimization was conducted by positioning the NH4 + at an approximate distance of 2.5 Å from the adsorbent over the hollow site. Subsequently, electronic structure calculations were performed at the CAM-B3LYP-D3/6–31 G(d,p) level of theory in aqueous media. The optimized geometry and adsorption energies of the adsorbent (GQD-NX/GQD-OX)- adsorbate (NH4 + ion) complex are depicted in Fig. 2 and Fig. 3, while Table S1 presents the complexes' intermolecular distances. The negative adsorption energy confirms the exothermic nature of the adsorption, signifying favourable adsorption ability and stability. The adsorption energy (Eads) of the NH4 + ion on the adsorbent is calculated using the provided formula.,
(1) Eads = Ecomplex – (Eadsorbent+ Eadsorbate)

The adsorption energy (Eads) represents the energy of adsorption of adsorbate onto the surface of adsorbent. Ecomplex is the energy of adsorbate-adsorbent complex, Eadsorbent is the energy of GQD-NX and GQD-OX and Eadsorbate is the energy of NH4 + ion. Negative adsorption energy indicates the favourable adsorption of the NH4 + ion on the surface of GQD-NX/GQD-OX.

The adsorption behaviour of NH4 + ion on GQD adsorbent along with its heteroatom doped counterparts i.e. GQD-NX and GQD-OX has been investigated. The analysis has revealed that the physisorption of NH4 + on GQD, primarily driven by van der Waals (vdW) interaction, did not cause any structural alterations, preserving the planar structure of the GQD- NH4 + ion complex. The weak interaction was supported by the adsorption energy of −0.17 eV and a significant adsorption distance ranging from 2.6 to 2.7 Å (Table S1). Further, for NH4 + @ GQD-NX complex, as the nitrogen content increases from two to six, there is an increase in the adsorption energy, leading to more stable adsorbate-adsorbent complex. It follows the order: NH4 + @ GQD-N6 (-1.79 eV) > NH4 + @ GQD-N5 (-1.23 eV) > NH4 + @ GQD-N4 (-1.15 eV) > NH4 + @ GQD-N3 (-0.91 eV) > NH4 + @ GQD-N2 (-0.34 eV). However, due to strong N-H hydrogen bonding interaction, there is exceptionally high adsorption energy for NH4 + @ GQD-N1 with −2.24 eV. Thus, the interaction of NH4 + ion with GQD-NX is primarily chemisorption except for GQD-N2. Similarly, for NH4 + @ GQD-OX, there is an increase in adsorption energy as the oxygen content increases from one to six, leading to better adsorption with increased O concentration. The adsorption energy follows the order NH4 + @ GQD-O6 (-1.05 eV) > NH4 + @ GQD-O4 (-0.62 eV) > NH4 + @ GQD-O5 (-0.5 eV) > NH4 + @ GQD-O3 (-0.4 eV) > NH4 + @ GQD-O2 (-0.37 eV) > NH4 + @ GQD-O1 (-0.28 eV). Hence, the interaction of NH4 + ions towards GQD-OX mainly ranges from strong physisorption to weak chemisorption except for GQD-O6. Analysis of the adsorption of NH4 + ions on GQD-NX and GQD-OX shows that in the optimized geometries, the N-H bonds consistently point towards the vacancy/doping site. The observed range of the shortest adsorption bond length, spanning from 1.7 to 2.5 Å, indicates the presence of weak and strong hydrogen bonding interactions within these systems. The comprehensive analysis indicates that GQD-NX exhibits a stronger binding affinity towards NH4 + ions when compared to GQD-OX.

Furthermore, to gain a more comprehensive understanding of the adsorption process, an ADCH charge analysis has also been performed to track changes in charge distribution on the NH4 + ion in the adsorbent-adsorbate complex compared to the bare ion. Table 1 reveals a significant decrease in the charges on the NH4 + ion, particularly on the GQD-NX adsorbent rather than the GQD-OX. This suggests a significant charge transfer from the GQD-NX/GQD-OX to the NH4 + ion. The introduction of vacancies and doping of heteroatoms with high electronegativity values in GQDs lead to a notable increase in the adsorption energy of the NH4 + ion, transitioning from a physiosorbed state to weak chemisorbed or strong chemisorbed state. These changes in the nature of the interaction between the NH4 + ion and adsorbent can be attributed to the redistribution of components contributing to the total adsorption energy. It is reasonable to assume that the NH4 + ion and pristine GQD and GQD-OX interaction occurs primarily through van der Waals forces and weak hydrogen bonds except for GQD-O6. In contrast, the interactions of GQD-NX with NH4 + is primarily governed by significant electrostatic interactions with strong hydrogen bonds in most of the cases.

The non-covalent interaction (NCI) provides a comprehensive and detailed analysis of the interaction between adsorbate and adsorbent. Understanding the strength of interaction at the adsorbate-adsorbent interface is of great importance. The NCI differentiates between steric hindrance, van der Waals interactions (dispersion interaction), and hydrogen bonds. These are represented by red, green, and blue patches in the 3D iso-surface and 2D reduced density gradient (RDG) scatter graphs, respectively.[40]. The NCI scatter graph illustrates the RDG as a function of sign(λ2) ρ(r), derived from density and its first derivative[41–43].
(6)
RDG
r
=
1
2
( 3
π
2
)
1 3
| ∇ ρ
r
|
ρ
r
4 ∕ 3
Where ∇, ρ(r), and |∇ρ(r)| are gradient operator, electron density and modular arithmetic of gradient operator for electron density, respectively[44]. In the RDG scatter graph, the range of sign(λ2) ρ(r) is between −0.05 and 0.05 a.u. The visualization of these weak interactions was achieved using the NCI 3D iso-surface plots and the 2D-RDG scatter plots, for the interaction between NH4 + and adsorbents i.e. GQD-NX/GQD-OX as depicted in Fig. 4 and Fig. 5, respectively. By examining the color-filled NCI-RDG iso-surface, we can differentiate between different regions. The RDG plot reveals that spikes occurring at more negative values of sign(λ2) ρ(r), which correspond to a denser accumulation of ρ(r), indicate stronger hydrogen bond interactions. This study uses the criteria of sign(λ2) ρ(r) < −0.01 to determine the strength of the hydrogen bond. However, values ranging between −0.01 < sign(λ2) ρ(r) < 0.00 are associated with very weak interactions, specifically vdW interactions[45].

For complexes involving NH4 +@GQD-NX, as illustrated in Fig. 4, the three-dimensional iso-surface of the NCI depicts the presence of flaky greenish and bluish patches. These patches can be attributed to the existence of van der Waals (vdW) and electrostatic forces, respectively. When the number of nitrogen concentration increases from two to six, the density of green patches decreases while the density of blue patches increases, a trend that is also evident in the two-dimensional RDG scatter graph. Specifically, for NH4 +@GQD-N3, the greenish spike ranges from ‐0.02 to 0.01 a.u. This observation suggests that the adsorption of NH4 + is primarily influenced by dispersion interaction (vdW) with a minor contribution from electrostatic forces. However, for NH4 +@GQD-N4 and NH4 +@GQD-N5, the contribution of electrostatic forces increases, as evidenced through the density of blue patches in the three-dimensional iso-surface and the blue spikes in the two-dimensional RDG map. These values range from −0.04 a.u. to beyond −0.05 a.u. For, NH4 +@GQD-N1, the deep greenish flaky patches together with blue patches indicate that the electrostatic forces dominate along with small contribution form van der Walls forces. Finally, for the NH4 +@GQD-N6 complex, the blue patch in the three-dimensional iso-surface and the intense blue peak at −0.035 a.u. in the two-dimensional scatter map indicates the presence of strong hydrogen bonding. This is attributed to the coulombic interactions between the electronegative N atom of GQD-N6 and the electropositive H atom of NH4 +.

In the analysis of complexes involving NH4 +@GQD-OX, as shown in Fig. 5, the three-dimensional iso-surface of the NCI indicates the presence of flaky greenish and bluish patches. The density of green patches decreases while the density of blue patches increases as the number of oxygen concentration increases from one to six. For the NH4 +@GQD-O1 complex, the green spikes range from ‐0.02 to 0.01 a.u., suggesting dominant dispersion forces. However, for NH4 +@GQD-O2, NH4 +@GQD-O3, NH4 +@GQD-O4 and NH4 +@GQD-O5 the density of green spikes increases within the range of ‐0.02 to 0.01 a.u., and the density of blue spikes appears around −0.05 to −0.02 a.u., indicating the presence of vdW interaction and weak hydrogen bonds. This observation is also supported by the green and blue patches in the 3D iso-surfaces of GQD-O2, GQD-O3, GQD-O4 and GQD-O5 complexes with NH4 +, respectively. Finally, for and NH4 +@GQD-O6, strong blue spike at −0.03 a.u. suggest strong hydrogen bonding due to the electronegativity difference between O and H atoms. The green and blue patches around the 3D iso-surfaces confirm this finding. Remarkably, the results obtained through NCI analysis align with the adsorption energy analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantum theory of atoms in molecules analysis
ce:section.ce:para:
The Quantum Theory of Atoms in Molecules (QTAIM) technique, pioneered by Richard F.W. Bader et al., serves as a pivotal method for topological assessment, facilitating the determination of bond types between molecules within a given system [46,47]. The electron density
ρ
r
at the bond critical point (BCP) stands as a crucial variable to examine non-covalent interactions in various molecular systems. QTAIM analysis encompasses the utilization of various topological factors to discern the nature of molecule-to-molecule interaction within the system under investigation. These variables include the density of all electrons
ρ
r
, Laplacian of electron density
∇
2
ρ
r
, Lagrangian kinetic energy
G
r
, Potential energy density
V
r
, Energy density
H
r
. The Laplacian of electron density is defined as the sum of the eigen value of the Hessian matrix[48].
(7)
∇
2
ρ
r
=
λ
1
+
λ
2
+
λ
3

The BCP with
∇
2
ρ
r
> 0 and
H
r
> 0 indicates a non-covalent nature of interactions,
∇
2
ρ
r
> 0 and
H
r
< 0 indicates a partial covalent nature of interaction, while
∇
2
ρ
r
< 0 and
H
r
< 0 indicates a strong covalent type of interaction. The energy density
H
r
can calculated by the formula given below:
(8)
H
r
=
G
r
+ V
r

Furthermore, the interatomic interactions can be estimated through the ratio of (-G (r))/ (V (r)). The values of (-G (r))/ (V (r)) < 0.5, (-G (r))/ (V (r)) > 0.5 and (-G (r))/ (V (r)) >1 corresponds to a covalent interaction, partial covalent interaction, and non-covalent interaction respectively [49]

Different BCPs for all complexes are observed which indicates the presence of different types of interactions between adsorbate and adsorbent. The adsorption of NH4 + molecule on pristine GQD reveals two BCP, which consist of two C – H bond interactions as shown in Table 2. The
∇
2
ρ
r
> 0 and
H
r
> 0 indicates a non-covalent nature of interactions, which also gets confirmation from the ratio of (-G (r))/ (V (r)) being greater than 1. Now considering the adsorption of NH4 + ion onto the surface of the GQD-N1 and GQD-N2 adsorbent, it reveals one critical point for each complex, which consist of N – H bond interaction. For both the cases the
∇
2
ρ
r
> 0 and
H
r
< 0, which suggests partial covalent interaction. In case of adsorption of NH4 + ion onto the surface of GQD-N3, GQD-N4, GQD-N5 and GQD-N6 adsorbents, there exists three, four, three and six BCPs respectively. The highest number of BCPs, i.e., 6 for NH4 +@GQD-N6 complex suggests the highest stability among all NH4 +@GQD-NX complex. These BCPs consists of N – H and N – N type of interactions. Presence of partial covalent interaction between the hydrogen atom of NH4 + and nitrogen atom of GQD-NX (X=4–6) is further confirmed with the presence of the
∇
2
ρ
r
> 0 and
H
r
< 0 for all N-H type interactions. Additionally, the ratio of (-G (r))/ (V (r)) lies in the range from 0.82 to 0.96, also suggests partial covalent nature.

Further, considering adsorption of NH4 + in GQD-OX, as reported in Table 3. The GQD-O1, GQD-O2, GQD-O3 form one BCP each, which consist of O – H bond interaction. For all three cases the
∇
2
ρ
r
> 0 and
H
r
< 0, which suggests partial covalent interaction between NH4 + and OGQD. For the remaining GQD-O4, GQD-O5 and GQD-O6 there exists four, five and six BCPs, which suggests increase in interaction with increasing number of oxygen atoms doped in GQD-OX. These BCPs consists of O – H and O – N type of interactions. The
∇
2
ρ
r
> 0 and
H
r
< 0 for O – H type of interaction, which suggests partial covalent interaction between NH4 + and GQD-OX (X=4–6). The ratio of (-G (r))/ (V (r)) for the above-mentioned BCPs, ranges between 0.9693 and 0.9864, also confirming the presence of partial covalent interactions. Moreover, the ratio of (-G (r))/ (V (r)) for GQD-OX describes the weaker adsorbate-adsorbent interactions in comparison to GQD-NX. Thus, the analysis of topological parameters suggests the existence of dispersion forces with some electrostatic interactions at some bond critical points. The NH4 +@GQD-N6 and NH4 +@GQD-O6 complexes are the most stable among all studied complexes with stronger interactions. The results of QTAIM are very well consistent with the NCI analysis.

An analysis of the density of states (DOS) was carried out to study the adsorption of NH4 + onto the adsorbent surfaces i.e. GQD-NX and GQD-OX, and the findings are presented in Fig. 6. Each vertical line in a DOS diagram corresponds to a molecular orbital. The dotted line denotes the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO). The total density-of-states (TDOS) is represented by the black line, while the contribution of the NH4 + ion and the adsorbent to TDOS is depicted by the blue and red lines, respectively. The adsorbent’s contribution to the TDOS is greater than that of the NH4 + ion across the entire energy range, as shown in Fig. 6. When NH4 + is adsorbed onto the adsorbent (GQD-NX and GQD-OX) surface, the complex structure primarily influences the TDOS from −20 eV to 10 eV. Additionally, NH4 + contributes to the TDOS between −19 eV and −17 eV, as well as 2 eV and 7 eV. The PDOS offers valuable insights into the specific impact of each fragment on the overall change in the energy gap of the complexes under study. Fig. 6 shows that both the NH4 + ion and the adsorbents have contributed to the TDOS. Furthermore, the TDOS spectra indicate that the peak intensity is greater in energy compared to the individual peaks of GQD-NX and GQD-OX, suggesting the generation of new energy levels by the adsorbed NH4 + ion.

The duration required for the desorption of adsorbate from the surface of the adsorbent is known as desorption time (τ). It can be determined by utilizing the Van't-Hoff-Arrhenius equation and the transition state theory [50].
(9)
τ =
A
- 1
exp
− E
a
kT

Here, A represents the apparent frequency factor expressed in units of 1012 s−1. The term Ea denotes the desorption energy barrier, which is assumed to be equivalent to the Eads value, as desorption is the reverse process of adsorption. Moreover, T is the operational temperature, while k corresponds to the Boltzmann constant, quantified as 8.62 × 10−5 eV K−1. The desorption time reported in Fig. 7 is calculated using the adsorption energies depicted in Fig. 2 and Fig. 3. The desorption times of NH4 + ions on the surfaces of the GQD-NX and GQD-OX adsorbents were calculated at 298 K. Due to physisorption and weak chemisorption of the NH4 + ions on the surface of GQD-N2 and GQD-N3, the desorption times ranged from microseconds to kiloseconds. Conversely, due to the chemisorption of NH4 + ions on the surfaces of other GQD-NX (X=1,4,5,6), the desorption time was relatively large, indicating that NH4 + ions had strongly adsorbed to their surfaces. These systems could be adsorbents to remove NH4 + ions from specific environments. Additionally, NH4 + ions physisorbed onto the surfaces of GQD-OX (X=1–5), resulting in desorption times in nanoseconds to milliseconds. The short recovery time makes these surfaces suitable as reusable adsorbent materials. The chemisorption of NH4 + ions onto the surface of GQD-O6 results in a longer desorption time, thereby significantly enhancing the effectiveness of this adsorbent in removing the same ion from a specific environment. The findings of the present study are consistent with the work of Farahani et al. [51], who reported a desorption time of 926 seconds for NH4 + ions on the surface of carbon nanotubes.

The adsorption mechanism of the ammonium (NH4 +) ion towards the surface of GQDs modified with vacancy and heteroatom (nitrogen (N) and oxygen (O)) doping in varying concentrations has been reported. All the calculations have been performed at the CAM-B3LYP level using a combination of dispersion-corrected density functional theory (DFT) and quantum theory of atoms in molecules (QTAIM) approaches in aqueous media. Adsorption energies, molecular electrostatic potential, charge transfer, density-of-states, non-covalent interaction, and desorption time have been calculated to deeply understand the influence of the adsorption mechanism of NH4 + ions onto the modified adsorbent (GQD-NX and GQD-OX) surfaces. The adsorption of NH4 + ions on GQD-NX surfaces involves primarily chemisorption, driven by electrostatic interactions and hydrogen bonds. In contrast, the adsorption of NH4 + on GQD-OX surfaces ranges from strong physisorption to weak chemisorption, predominantly due to van der Waals interactions and hydrogen bonds. This study has revealed that the GQD-NX exhibited superior adsorption capabilities compared to the GQD-OX for molecular adsorption of NH4 + ion. The adsorption energy changes based on the concentration of doped heteroatoms. The present research suggests an innovative approach that utilizes modified GQDs as highly efficient adsorbents for removing NH4 + ions from water.
Not applicable.

Saurav Kumar: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Neha Agnihotri: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Software, Resources, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the Science and Engineering Research Board (SERB), New Delhi, India, for financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.mtcomm.2024.110366.
